title: Python Fundamentals
description: The world's most popular language. Syntax, data types, control flow, functions.

questions.q: What is Python?
options: A high-level, interpreted, general-purpose programming language known for readable syntax
A compiled low-level language like C
questions.explanation: Python's design philosophy emphasizes readability. It's the most-used language for AI, data science, scripting, and automation.

questions.q: Who created Python?
options: Guido van Rossum, first released in 1991
questions.explanation: Guido created Python as a successor to ABC language. The name is from Monty Python's Flying Circus, not the snake.

questions.q: Which is a TRUE statement about Python?
options: It is dynamically typed — you don't declare variable types explicitly
It requires variable type declarations like Java
It must be compiled before running
questions.explanation: Type inference at runtime makes Python flexible but means errors that other languages catch at compile time appear at runtime.

questions.q: Why is Python especially popular for AI and data science?
options: Rich ecosystem (NumPy, Pandas, PyTorch, TensorFlow), readable syntax, and massive community
It's the only language that supports AI
questions.explanation: Python won AI/data dominance through libraries, not speed. The heavy computation happens in C/C++/CUDA under the hood.

questions.q: What does 'Pythonic' mean?
options: Code that follows Python's idioms and style conventions — clean, readable, expressive
questions.explanation: The Zen of Python (`import this`) outlines the principles. 'There should be one obvious way to do it' is the most quoted.

questions.q: What does Python use to define code blocks?
questions.explanation: No braces, no semicolons. Indentation IS the syntax. Mixing tabs and spaces causes IndentationError.

questions.q: Which is the CORRECT way to write an if statement?
questions.explanation: Colon ends the condition; the next line is indented. No parentheses required around the condition.

questions.q: How do you write a single-line comment in Python?
questions.explanation: # starts a single-line comment. Triple quotes ''' ''' are technically strings but commonly used as multi-line docs.

questions.q: Are semicolons required at the end of Python statements?
options: No — newlines end statements (though semicolons CAN separate multiple statements on one line)
Only at the end of functions
Only in scripts, not in modules
questions.explanation: Python uses newlines as statement terminators. Putting multiple statements on one line with ; is legal but unpythonic.

questions.q: What is PEP 8?
options: The official Python style guide — covers naming, spacing, line length, and conventions
questions.explanation: PEP 8 is the most influential PEP. Tools like Black, Ruff, and flake8 enforce or autoformat to PEP 8 conventions.

questions.q: How do you assign a value to a variable in Python?
questions.explanation: No declaration keyword and no type annotation needed. Python figures out the type from the value.

questions.q: What are the BASIC numeric types in Python?
questions.explanation: Python's int has arbitrary precision — no overflow on huge numbers. float follows IEEE 754 (same precision quirks as JS/Java).

questions.q: What does `type(5)` return?
questions.explanation: type() returns the class object. Use isinstance(x, int) for type checks in production code (it respects inheritance).

questions.q: What's the difference between `True` and `true` in Python?
options: Only `True` (capitalized) is a valid boolean — Python is case-sensitive
true is for older Python versions
questions.explanation: Common gotcha for JS/Java devs. Python's booleans are True and False (capital T/F). Also: None, not null.

questions.q: What is `None` in Python?
options: A singleton representing the absence of a value (similar to null in other languages)
questions.explanation: Check with `x is None`, never `x == None`. There's only one None object in memory; identity check is faster and idiomatic.

questions.q: Which is a VALID way to create a string in Python?
questions.explanation: Single and double quotes are interchangeable. Triple-quoted '''...''' allow multi-line strings.

questions.q: What is an f-string?
options: A string literal prefixed with f that allows {expression} interpolation: f'Hi {name}'
A formatted string from a file
questions.explanation: f-strings (Python 3.6+) are the modern, fastest way to format strings. Far more readable than % or .format().

questions.q: What does `'hello'.upper()` return?
options: 'HELLO' — strings have methods like upper(), lower(), strip(), split()
questions.explanation: Strings are IMMUTABLE in Python. All string methods return a new string; they never modify the original.

questions.q: How do you check if a substring exists in a string?
questions.explanation: The `in` operator is the most pythonic. Also works on lists, dicts (checks keys), sets, and any iterable.

questions.q: What does `'a-b-c'.split('-')` return?
questions.explanation: split converts string → list. The inverse is '-'.join(['a','b','c']) → 'a-b-c'. Master both.

questions.q: How do you create a list in Python?
questions.explanation: Square brackets create a list. Parentheses make a tuple. Curly braces make a set (or dict).

questions.q: What does `fruits.append('orange')` do?
options: Adds 'orange' to the end of the list, modifying it in place (returns None)
questions.explanation: Many list methods mutate in place and return None. Common bug: `fruits = fruits.append(x)` → fruits becomes None.

questions.q: How do you access the LAST item in a list?
questions.explanation: Negative indices count from the end. [-1] is last, [-2] is second-to-last. Very pythonic.

questions.q: What does `nums[1:4]` do (slicing)?
options: Returns a new list with elements at index 1, 2, 3 (4 is excluded)
questions.explanation: Slicing is end-exclusive. [start:stop:step]. nums[::-1] is the classic reverse-a-list trick.

questions.q: What is a LIST COMPREHENSION?
options: A concise way to build a new list: [x*2 for x in nums if x > 0]
questions.explanation: List comprehensions are pythonic, fast, and readable for simple transformations. Don't nest them deeply — clarity matters.

questions.q: How do you create a dictionary in Python?
questions.explanation: Curly braces with key:value pairs. Also: dict(name='Ahmad', age=17). Keys must be hashable (strings, numbers, tuples).

questions.q: How do you access a dictionary value by key?
questions.explanation: user['name'] raises KeyError if missing; user.get('name') returns None. get also accepts a default: user.get('age', 0).

questions.q: What does `user.get('age', 0)` do?
options: Returns user['age'] if it exists, otherwise 0
questions.explanation: Always prefer .get() with a default when a key may be missing. Avoids try/except KeyError boilerplate.

questions.q: How do you ITERATE over key-value pairs?
questions.explanation: .items() is the cleanest way. Iterating `for k in dict` gives keys only — useful but easy to confuse with values.

questions.q: What's a DICT COMPREHENSION?
options: {key: value for key, value in pairs}
questions.explanation: Same idea as list comprehensions but for dicts. Example: {n: n*n for n in range(5)} → {0:0, 1:1, 2:4, 3:9, 4:16}.

questions.q: What is a TUPLE in Python?
options: An immutable ordered collection: (1, 2, 3) — can't be modified after creation
questions.explanation: Use tuples for fixed collections (coordinates, RGB values, function returns). Faster than lists and hashable.

questions.q: What is the MAIN difference between a list and a tuple?
options: Lists are mutable (can be changed); tuples are immutable (fixed once created)
questions.explanation: Choose by intent. Mutating sequence → list. Fixed record-like data → tuple. Tuples can also be dict keys; lists can't.

questions.q: What is a SET in Python?
options: An unordered collection of UNIQUE items: {1, 2, 3}
questions.explanation: Sets are perfect for deduplication and membership tests (O(1) lookup vs O(n) for lists). Use set(list) to dedupe quickly.

questions.q: What does `set([1,2,2,3,3])` return?
questions.explanation: Classic deduplication idiom. Note: sets don't preserve insertion order (though they do in CPython 3.7+ implementation detail).

questions.q: What operations do sets SUPPORT that lists don't?
questions.explanation: Sets shine for set algebra. a & b → items in both. a | b → items in either. a - b → items in a but not b.

questions.q: Which is the CORRECT if/elif/else syntax?
options: if x > 0 else if x == 0 else
questions.explanation: elif is Python's 'else if'. Note the colons after each condition. No parentheses needed around conditions.

questions.q: What does this loop do? `for i in range(5):`
questions.explanation: range(stop) → 0 to stop-1. range(start, stop) → start to stop-1. range(start, stop, step) for full control.

questions.q: What's a `while` loop in Python?
options: Runs as long as a condition is True: while count < 10: count += 1
questions.explanation: Use while when you don't know the iteration count in advance. Always include something that changes the condition to avoid infinite loops.

questions.q: What does `break` do inside a loop?
questions.explanation: break exits early. continue skips to the next iteration. pass does nothing (useful as a placeholder).

questions.q: What's the PYTHONIC way to loop with an index AND value?
questions.explanation: enumerate is one of the most underused built-ins. Cleaner than manual index tracking.

questions.q: How do you DEFINE a function?
options: def add(a, b):\n    return a + b
function add(a, b) { return a + b }
questions.explanation: def keyword, function name, parameters in parens, colon, indented body. return is optional (defaults to None).

questions.q: What's a DEFAULT parameter value?
options: A value used if the caller doesn't provide that argument: def greet(name='World'): ...
questions.explanation: Defaults make arguments optional. WARNING: never use mutable defaults like [] or {} — they're shared across calls (classic gotcha).

questions.q: What are KEYWORD ARGUMENTS?
options: Passing arguments by name: greet(name='Ahmad') instead of relying on position
questions.explanation: Keyword args make function calls self-documenting. Especially valuable with functions that have many parameters.

questions.q: What is `*args` in a function definition?
options: Collects all extra positional arguments into a tuple
questions.explanation: **kwargs does the same for keyword arguments (into a dict). Together they let functions accept any number of args.

questions.q: What is a LAMBDA function?
options: A small anonymous function: lambda x: x * 2
questions.explanation: Lambdas are limited to a single expression. Great for short callbacks (map, filter, sorted with a key). For anything bigger, use def.

questions.q: How do you IMPORT a module?
questions.explanation: Imports give access to module contents via dot notation. Always at the top of the file by convention (PEP 8).

questions.q: What does `from math import sqrt` do?
options: Imports only sqrt into the namespace — call as sqrt(4) instead of math.sqrt(4)
questions.explanation: Cleaner for frequently-used names. Use sparingly to avoid namespace pollution. Avoid `from X import *` in production code.

questions.q: What does `import numpy as np` do?
options: Imports numpy and gives it the local alias np for shorter code
questions.explanation: Standard convention in data science: numpy as np, pandas as pd, matplotlib.pyplot as plt.

questions.q: What is a Python PACKAGE?
options: A folder containing Python modules (and an __init__.py file traditionally)
A zip file of Python code
questions.explanation: Packages organize modules hierarchically. In modern Python (3.3+), __init__.py is optional for namespace packages.

questions.q: What does `if __name__ == '__main__':` do?
options: Runs the block only when the file is executed directly, not when imported as a module
Required at the start of every file
questions.explanation: Lets a file work as both a script AND a reusable module. Idiomatic guard for entry-point code.

questions.q: What's the RECOMMENDED way to open a file?
options: with open('file.txt') as f:\n    data = f.read()
questions.explanation: The with statement (context manager) auto-closes the file even on errors. Never use bare open() in production code.

questions.q: What does `open('data.txt', 'r')` mean?
options: Opens data.txt in READ mode (default if mode is omitted)
questions.explanation: Modes: 'r' read, 'w' write (truncates!), 'a' append, 'b' binary, '+' read-write. e.g., 'rb' = read binary.

questions.q: How do you read a JSON file?
options: import json; with open('file.json') as f: data = json.load(f)
JSON requires a separate library install
questions.explanation: json is a built-in standard-library module. json.load reads from file; json.loads parses a string.

questions.q: What's the difference between `read()` and `readlines()`?
options: read() returns the whole file as one string; readlines() returns a list of lines
read() is for binary, readlines() for text
questions.explanation: For huge files, iterate the file object directly: `for line in f:` — memory-efficient, doesn't load the whole file.

questions.q: How do you WRITE to a file?
questions.explanation: Mode 'w' truncates the file first! Use 'a' to append. Always use the with statement to ensure proper closing.

questions.q: How do you handle an exception in Python?
questions.explanation: try/except/else/finally is Python's exception model. Always catch specific exceptions, not bare except (which hides bugs).

questions.q: Why is `except:` (bare except) considered BAD?
options: It catches EVERYTHING including KeyboardInterrupt and SystemExit, hiding real bugs
questions.explanation: Always except specific types: except (ValueError, KeyError). Use except Exception only at top-level error boundaries.

questions.q: What does the `finally` block do?
options: Runs whether the try succeeds, fails, or raises — useful for cleanup
questions.explanation: finally guarantees cleanup (close files, release locks). Modern code often prefers context managers (with) over try/finally.

questions.q: What does `raise ValueError('bad input')` do?
options: Raises a ValueError exception with the given message
questions.explanation: Use raise to signal errors. Raise specific built-in exceptions or define custom ones inheriting from Exception.

questions.q: What's the PYTHONIC philosophy for errors?
options: EAFP — 'Easier to Ask Forgiveness than Permission' (try/except over check-first)
questions.explanation: EAFP is more pythonic than LBYL ('Look Before You Leap'). Example: `try: d['key'] except KeyError:` over `if 'key' in d:`.

questions.q: How do you DEFINE a class?
options: class Dog:\n    def __init__(self, name):\n        self.name = name
questions.explanation: __init__ is the constructor. self is the instance reference (similar to this in other languages) — always the first parameter.

questions.q: What is `self` in a class method?
options: A reference to the current instance — must be the first parameter of instance methods
questions.explanation: self is convention, not syntax — you could name it anything, but don't. It's how methods access instance attributes.

questions.q: What's INHERITANCE?
options: A class can inherit attributes/methods from a parent: class Puppy(Dog): ...
questions.explanation: Inheritance enables code reuse and polymorphism. Python supports multiple inheritance (be careful with the method resolution order).

questions.q: What's a `@dataclass`?
options: A decorator (3.7+) that auto-generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__ from class attributes
questions.explanation: @dataclass eliminates boilerplate for value classes. Often preferred over plain classes for data-holding objects.

questions.q: What's a class with `__init__` and `__repr__` used for?
options: Creating objects (constructor) and defining how they print/show in the REPL
questions.explanation: Dunder (double underscore) methods customize built-in behavior: __len__, __iter__, __eq__, __add__, etc. Powerful.

questions.q: What is an ITERATOR in Python?
options: An object that produces values one at a time when next() is called on it
questions.explanation: All iterables (lists, dicts, files, generators) can become iterators. Iterators implement __iter__ and __next__.

questions.q: What is a GENERATOR function?
options: A function that uses `yield` to produce values lazily — one at a time, memory-efficient
A function that generates random numbers
questions.explanation: Generators are perfect for streams of data, infinite sequences, or large datasets where loading all in memory is wasteful.

questions.q: What's the difference between `return` and `yield`?
options: return ends the function; yield pauses it and returns a value, resuming on the next next() call
questions.explanation: yield turns a function into a generator. Each yield pauses execution and produces a value. Truly mind-expanding pattern.

questions.q: What's a GENERATOR EXPRESSION?
options: Like a list comprehension but with parentheses: (x*2 for x in nums)  — produces values lazily
questions.explanation: Use generator expressions when you don't need the full list in memory — e.g., sum(x*2 for x in big_data). Saves memory.

questions.q: When should you prefer a GENERATOR over a list?
options: When iterating once over potentially large data, or when memory matters
questions.explanation: Lists are random-access and reusable. Generators are one-pass and lazy. Choose based on access pattern + size.

questions.q: Which built-in module handles dates and times?
questions.explanation: datetime.date, datetime.time, datetime.datetime, datetime.timedelta. For timezone-aware ops use the zoneinfo module (3.9+).

questions.q: Which built-in module handles JSON?
options: json must be installed via pip
questions.explanation: Built-in. json.dumps (Python → str), json.loads (str → Python), json.dump/load for files.

questions.q: Which built-in module handles file/directory paths?
questions.explanation: pathlib.Path is the modern object-oriented API. Path('data') / 'file.txt' beats os.path.join() in readability.

questions.q: Which module helps with COLLECTIONS like default dicts and counters?
questions.explanation: collections is a treasure trove. Counter(['a','b','a']) → Counter({'a': 2, 'b': 1}). Saves so much boilerplate.

questions.q: Which built-in is for COMMAND-LINE arguments?
questions.explanation: argparse handles help text, type validation, subcommands, and defaults. For modern projects, also consider Typer or Click.

questions.q: What is a VIRTUAL ENVIRONMENT?
options: An isolated Python environment with its own packages, preventing version conflicts between projects
questions.explanation: Always use a virtualenv per project. Standard tool: python -m venv .venv. Activate with source .venv/bin/activate (Linux/Mac).

questions.q: What is pip?
options: Python's standard package installer — installs from PyPI (Python Package Index)
questions.explanation: pip install requests, pip uninstall x, pip list, pip freeze > requirements.txt. The backbone of Python project management.

questions.q: What's `requirements.txt` for?
options: Lists all packages (with versions) needed to run your project — others install via pip install -r requirements.txt
questions.explanation: Critical for reproducibility. Pin versions for production (requests==2.31.0) to avoid surprise breakages from upstream changes.

questions.q: What is `uv` (a modern Python tool)?
options: A fast, Rust-based package manager and virtualenv tool — a modern alternative to pip + venv
questions.explanation: uv is rapidly gaining adoption in 2025-2026 for being 10-100x faster than pip. Also consider Poetry and pdm for project management.

questions.q: Why should you NEVER `pip install` globally on a system?
options: It can corrupt system Python, cause version conflicts, and may require sudo (risky)
Pip doesn't have a global install option
questions.explanation: Always pip install inside an activated virtualenv. Or use pipx for installing CLI tools globally in isolated envs.

questions.q: Which library is THE standard for data analysis in Python?
questions.explanation: pandas provides DataFrames (2D labeled data) — Excel on steroids. Built on NumPy. The lingua franca of Python data work.

questions.q: What is NumPy used for?
options: Fast numerical computing on N-dimensional arrays — the foundation of scientific Python
questions.explanation: NumPy arrays are 10-100x faster than Python lists for math. Underlies pandas, PyTorch, TensorFlow, scikit-learn, and more.

questions.q: Which library makes HTTP REQUESTS to APIs?
options: requests (or httpx for modern async support)
questions.explanation: requests is the most-loved Python library. requests.get(url).json() is one line that solves what takes 10 in stdlib's urllib.

questions.q: Which library is the standard for MACHINE LEARNING in Python?
options: scikit-learn for classical ML; PyTorch/TensorFlow for deep learning
questions.explanation: scikit-learn covers regression, classification, clustering, preprocessing. PyTorch dominates research; both are widely used in industry.

questions.q: Which framework is most popular for WEB APIs in Python?
options: FastAPI (modern, async, typed) or Flask (simple, classic) or Django (full-stack)
questions.explanation: FastAPI exploded since 2019 thanks to type hints + auto OpenAPI docs + async speed. Django for full-stack apps. Flask for tiny services.

questions.q: What are TYPE HINTS in Python?
options: Optional annotations: def add(a: int, b: int) -> int: — checked by tools, not at runtime
questions.explanation: Type hints (PEP 484) enable IDE intelligence, mypy/pyright type checking, and self-documenting code. Embrace them in modern code.

questions.q: What does `match` do (Python 3.10+)?
options: Structural pattern matching — like a smarter switch statement: match x: case 1: ... case _: ...
questions.explanation: match/case can pattern-match against types, structures, and bindings. Much more powerful than switch in other languages.

questions.q: What does the walrus operator `:=` do (Python 3.8+)?
options: Assigns and returns a value in one expression: if (n := len(x)) > 10: ...
questions.explanation: Saves repetition in conditions and comprehensions. Use sparingly — overusing reduces readability.

questions.q: What are F-STRING formatting specs?
options: f'{value:.2f}' formats to 2 decimals; f'{x:>10}' pads right-aligned; f'{n:,}' adds thousand separators
questions.explanation: f-strings have full format spec mini-language. f'{pi=:.3f}' (3.8+) shows 'pi=3.142' — great for debugging.

questions.q: What is `async`/`await` for in Python?
options: Writing concurrent code that can wait on I/O without blocking — used with asyncio for async programs
questions.explanation: Best for I/O-bound concurrency (web requests, DB queries). For CPU-bound work, use multiprocessing instead.

questions.q: Why is `def f(items=[])` a CLASSIC BUG?
options: Default mutable arguments persist across calls — items keeps growing between calls
questions.explanation: Use def f(items=None): if items is None: items = []. One of the most-asked Python interview gotchas.

questions.q: What's a 'truthy' and 'falsy' value in Python?
options: Falsy: False, None, 0, '', [], {}, set(). Everything else is truthy.
questions.explanation: if my_list: works for 'non-empty'. Pythonic. Don't write if len(my_list) > 0 — Python idiom is the implicit truthiness check.

questions.q: What's the DIFFERENCE between `==` and `is`?
options: == compares values; `is` checks identity (same object in memory)
questions.explanation: Use `is None`, `is True`, `is False` (singletons). Use == for value comparison. Common bug: `x is 'hello'` works by accident due to string interning.

questions.q: Why use a LINTER and FORMATTER for Python?
options: Catch bugs early, enforce consistent style — tools: Ruff (fast linter), Black (formatter), mypy (type checker)
questions.explanation: Modern stack: Ruff (replaces flake8/isort/pylint, blazing fast) + Black or Ruff format + mypy/pyright for types.

questions.q: What's a HEALTHY rule for Python imports?
options: Group: stdlib, third-party, local — separated by blank lines (PEP 8)
Only at the bottom of files
One import per line is forbidden
questions.explanation: isort or Ruff can auto-sort imports. Always import at module top (avoid inside functions unless avoiding circular imports).

questions.q: What's the BEST way to learn Python deeply?
options: Build real projects, read the official docs, study other people's clean code, contribute to open source
questions.explanation: Python's official docs (docs.python.org) are excellent. The standard library tour alone is worth a weekend of study.

questions.q: Which type of projects accelerate Python learning?
options: Automate something annoying in your life — file renaming, web scraping, daily reports, API wrappers
Wait until you know everything before building
questions.explanation: 'Automate the Boring Stuff with Python' (free online book) is the canonical practical Python introduction.

questions.q: Which Python skill UNLOCKS the most career value?
options: Working with data (pandas + SQL + a visualization library) — data is everywhere
questions.explanation: Data analysis + automation = 80% of paid Python jobs. AI/ML is a strong second. Web dev a strong third.

questions.q: What's a great community for Python learners?
options: r/learnpython, Real Python, Python Discord, Talk Python To Me podcast, Stack Overflow
questions.explanation: Python has one of the friendliest tech communities. Ask questions early and often.

questions.q: What's the MOST important meta-skill for Python long-term?
options: Writing readable, maintainable code that others (and future you) can understand
questions.explanation: Code is read more than written. Readability is what makes Python Python. Internalize that and the rest follows.
